articles.title: Beyoncé Returns to Met Gala 2026 With Blue Ivy After a Decade Away
articles.summary: Beyoncé made her return to the Met Gala on Monday night for the first time in ten years, walking the cream carpet alongside her daughter Blue Ivy. The most Grammy-awarded musical artist in history showed up in a showstopping gown that instantly dominated the conversation. Blue Ivy joining her made the moment feel like a full-circle fashion debut.
articles.content:
Beyoncé was back at the Met Gala on Monday, 4 May 2026, and the fashion world felt it. Her last appearance at the event was a decade ago, which made this return one of the most anticipated moments of the entire night. She arrived in a showstopping gown that immediately became the image every outlet was racing to publish.

What made the moment even bigger was who she brought with her. Blue Ivy, Beyoncé and Jay-Z's eldest daughter, walked the cream carpet right beside her mother. Blue Ivy is 14 years old and has already won a Grammy of her own, for her vocal contribution to the song "Brown Skin Girl" back in 2021, so she is no stranger to big stages. But a Met Gala debut is its own kind of milestone.

For anyone who needs a quick explainer, the Met Gala is the annual fundraising event for the Metropolitan Museum of Art's Costume Institute in New York City. It is widely considered the biggest night in fashion, and an invitation is one of the most coveted things in celebrity culture. A ten-year absence from Beyoncé makes her return genuinely newsworthy, not just tabloid noise.

Beyoncé has been in an era of total cultural dominance lately, from the Cowboy Carter album cycle to her Renaissance World Tour breaking records. Seeing her step back onto the Met Gala carpet with Blue Ivy at her side felt less like a red carpet moment and more like a statement. The Carter women have arrived, together, and on their own terms.
keyPoints: Beyoncé attended the Met Gala for the first time in ten years
Blue Ivy walked the carpet alongside her mother in what was her own Met Gala debut
Blue Ivy is already a Grammy winner at age 14
Beyoncé is the most Grammy-awarded musical artist in history
The event is the annual fundraiser for the Metropolitan Museum of Art's Costume Institute

articles.title: Sabrina Carpenter and Stevie Nicks Sing Fleetwood Mac at Met Gala
articles.summary: Sabrina Carpenter performed at the 2026 Met Gala on Monday night and brought out Stevie Nicks for a mesmerizing duet of Fleetwood Mac's classic song Landslide. Carpenter also performed her own hits including Espresso, Please Please Please, and House Tour. It is already being called one of the standout moments of the night.
articles.content:
The 2026 Met Gala happened on Monday, May 4, and while the fashion is always the headline, this year Sabrina Carpenter gave everyone something else to talk about entirely. Carpenter performed at the event and at some point during her set, Stevie Nicks walked out and the two of them performed Fleetwood Mac's Landslide together. By all accounts, it stopped the room.

Landslide, for anyone who needs a refresher, is one of Fleetwood Mac's most beloved songs. Stevie Nicks wrote it in 1975 and it has been covered countless times, but hearing it live from Nicks herself alongside Carpenter is something genuinely special. Nicks is one of rock music's true icons, and her presence at the Met Gala was not widely expected, which made the moment hit even harder.

Carpenter also performed several of her own tracks during the night, including Espresso, Please Please Please, and House Tour. Those three songs became massive in 2024 and 2025, turning Carpenter from a well-liked pop artist into a genuine superstar. Getting to close out that kind of set by sharing the stage with Stevie Nicks is the sort of moment that defines a career chapter.

The Met Gala is the Metropolitan Museum of Art's annual fundraising event in New York, often described as fashion's biggest night. It doubles as a live performance space for a select few artists, making a set there a major stamp of cultural arrival. Sabrina Carpenter performing there in 2026, and pulling off a moment like this one, cements just how far she has come in a very short time.

People are already sharing clips across social media and the consensus is clear. This was one of the most memorable Met Gala performances in years. The combination of Carpenter's current pop dominance and Nicks's timeless presence created exactly the kind of unexpected, goosebump moment that live events are supposed to produce.
keyPoints: Sabrina Carpenter performed at the 2026 Met Gala on May 4
Stevie Nicks joined Carpenter on stage for a duet of Fleetwood Mac's Landslide
Carpenter also performed Espresso, Please Please Please, and House Tour
The Met Gala is fashion's biggest annual night, held at New York's Metropolitan Museum of Art
The performance is being widely praised as one of the night's standout moments

articles.title: X User Tricks Grok into Sending $200,000 in Crypto Using Morse Code
articles.summary: An X user found a way to trick Grok, the AI chatbot built by xAI, into sending around $200,000 worth of cryptocurrency by exploiting its connection to an automated trading bot, using Morse code to smuggle instructions past Grok's safety filters. The incident is a vivid example of a prompt injection attack, where hidden or disguised instructions fool an AI into doing something it should not. It raises serious questions about what happens when AI systems are connected to real money.
articles.content:
Someone on X just pulled off one of the wildest AI exploits in recent memory. A user managed to get Grok, the AI chatbot created by Elon Musk's company xAI, to send approximately $200,000 worth of crypto to their account. The method they used was Morse code.

Here is how it worked. Grok had been connected to an automated trading bot, meaning it had the ability to execute real financial transactions. The user sent Grok a message encoded in Morse code, which Grok translated and then followed as if it were a legitimate instruction. Because the message bypassed Grok's normal text-based safety checks in its encoded form, Grok did not flag it as suspicious. It just decoded the message and acted on it, sending the funds.

This is a textbook example of what security researchers call a prompt injection attack. The idea is simple: you disguise an instruction in a way the AI does not recognise as a command at the surface level, but the AI processes it anyway and does what it says. Researchers have been warning about this kind of attack for years, especially as AI systems get connected to tools that can take real actions in the real world, like sending emails, making purchases, or in this case moving cryptocurrency.

The Grok incident makes the risk concrete in a way that is hard to ignore. Two hundred thousand dollars is not a test scenario or a theoretical number. That is real money that left someone's account because an AI chatbot was fooled by dots and dashes. It is a reminder that connecting any AI to financial systems, without extremely robust safeguards, is a genuinely dangerous idea right now.

xAI has not yet made a detailed public statement about the exploit or confirmed whether the funds were recovered. The incident is already spreading fast across tech and crypto communities. Expect it to become a case study in AI safety discussions for a long time. The question everyone is now asking is how many other AI systems are sitting on top of automated tools, waiting for someone creative enough to find the back door.
keyPoints: An X user tricked Grok into sending roughly $200,000 in cryptocurrency using a Morse code prompt injection attack
Grok had been linked to an automated trading bot, which gave it the ability to move real funds
The Morse code encoding allowed the malicious instruction to slip past Grok's standard safety filters
This is a real-world example of a prompt injection attack, a known AI security risk
xAI has not confirmed whether the transferred funds were recovered

articles.title: Rihanna in Margiela and Cardi B in Marc Jacobs: Met Gala 2026 Best Looks
articles.summary: The 2026 Met Gala cream carpet delivered some genuinely wild fashion moments, with Rihanna in Maison Margiela, A$AP Rocky in Chanel and Cardi B in Marc Jacobs leading the pack. This year's event brought out looks that ranged from sculptural to surreal, giving fashion fans plenty to debate. Here are the outfits that actually stopped the scroll.
articles.content:
Every year the Met Gala produces a handful of looks that become the cultural shorthand for that moment in fashion, and 2026 was no different. The cream carpet, which replaced the traditional red carpet at this venue, was packed with choices that swung from breathtaking to bewildering. That is exactly what the night is for.

Rihanna turned up in Maison Margiela, which felt like a natural pairing given that Maison Margiela's creative director John Galliano has built the house into one of the most conceptually daring in the industry. Rihanna has always treated the Met Gala as her personal runway and this year was no exception. A$AP Rocky went in a completely different direction, choosing Chanel, and the pairing of his streetwear-rooted aesthetic with one of fashion's most classic houses made for one of the more talked-about looks of the night.

Cardi B showed up in Marc Jacobs, a designer who has always leaned into drama and excess, which makes him a natural fit for her energy. Marc Jacobs has a long history with the Met Gala, having dressed some of fashion's biggest moments over the years, so seeing Cardi B in his work was a high-voltage combination.

The Met Gala is not just about who wore what. It is about who took a risk, who committed fully to the theme, and who managed to make fashion feel like it actually means something. The 2026 edition had no shortage of stars willing to go all in. These looks will be the ones people are still referencing six months from now when they are talking about what this moment in fashion felt like.
keyPoints: Rihanna wore Maison Margiela to the 2026 Met Gala
A$AP Rocky wore Chanel, blending his streetwear roots with a classic fashion house
Cardi B wore Marc Jacobs on the cream carpet
The Met Gala cream carpet is considered the biggest fashion moment of the year
These looks are already dominating cultural conversation online

articles.title: Christopher Nolan Drops First Odyssey Trailer With Damon and Holland
articles.summary: Christopher Nolan has released a new trailer for his adaptation of Homer's The Odyssey, showing Matt Damon as Odysseus, Tom Holland as his son Telemachus, and Anne Hathaway as Penelope. This is one of the most anticipated films of the year, marking Nolan's first dive into ancient mythology. The trailer is already generating serious buzz online.
articles.content:
Christopher Nolan has just dropped a new trailer for The Odyssey, his big-screen adaptation of Homer's ancient Greek epic, and it looks absolutely stunning. The trailer gives us our first proper look at Matt Damon as Odysseus, the legendary hero who spends years trying to get home after the fall of Troy. It also shows Tom Holland as Telemachus, Odysseus's son who grows up waiting for a father who never seems to arrive, and Anne Hathaway as Penelope, the loyal wife holding everything together back home.

If you are not familiar with The Odyssey, here is the short version. It is one of the oldest and most famous stories in Western literature, written around 800 BC, and it follows Odysseus on a decade-long journey home filled with monsters, gods, and impossible choices. Nolan adapting it is a huge deal because this is the director behind Inception, Interstellar, Dunkirk, and Oppenheimer, films known for their scale, ambition, and emotional punch.

The casting alone has people talking. Damon brings a weathered, grounded energy that feels right for a man who has seen too much and just wants to get home. Holland, coming off his run as Spider-Man in the Marvel universe, is stepping into very different territory here, and the trailer suggests he is more than up for it. Hathaway's Penelope looks commanding and quietly fierce, which tracks perfectly with how the character is written in Homer's original poem.

There is no confirmed release date in the trailer, but the fact that Nolan is releasing new footage now suggests the film is not far off. Given that Oppenheimer became a genuine cultural phenomenon in 2023, the pressure and excitement around The Odyssey are enormous. This is shaping up to be one of those must-see cinema events.
keyPoints: A new trailer for Christopher Nolan's The Odyssey is now online
Matt Damon plays Odysseus, Tom Holland plays his son Telemachus, and Anne Hathaway plays Penelope
The Odyssey is based on Homer's ancient Greek epic about a hero's decade-long journey home
Nolan is the director behind Oppenheimer, Interstellar, and Inception
No official release date has been confirmed yet

articles.title: Steven Soderbergh Used Meta AI to Rebuild John Lennon for New Doc
articles.summary: Director Steven Soderbergh has made an AI-assisted documentary about John Lennon, working directly with Meta to create it. The film will premiere at the Cannes Film Festival later this month. It is one of the highest-profile uses of AI to reconstruct a deceased music legend on screen.
articles.content:
Steven Soderbergh is one of the most restless directors working today, known for jumping between big studio films, experimental projects, and everything in between. His credits include the Ocean's trilogy, Traffic, and the pandemic thriller Contagion. Now he has made a documentary about John Lennon, and he built it with help from Meta's AI tools.

The film will debut at the Cannes Film Festival, which runs later this month. Cannes is the world's most prestigious film festival, held annually in the south of France, and a premiere there puts this project right at the centre of the global conversation about AI and creative work.

The specific details of how Meta's AI was used have not been fully laid out yet, but the collaboration is significant. Meta has been pushing hard into AI-generated media, and working with a filmmaker of Soderbergh's stature gives that effort serious cultural credibility. John Lennon, who was murdered in New York City in December 1980, has remained one of the most iconic figures in music history, which means any AI reconstruction of his voice or likeness will draw enormous scrutiny.

This comes at a moment when the music and film industries are wrestling hard with questions about using AI to resurrect deceased artists. The Beatles' team used AI to complete the track Now and Then in 2023, which became the band's first new song in decades. That project was mostly celebrated. How audiences and critics respond to Soderbergh's Lennon documentary may help set the tone for what comes next.

The Cannes premiere will be the real test. Soderbergh has enough credibility to get a fair hearing, but this is exactly the kind of project that will spark fierce debate about consent, legacy, and who gets to decide how a dead artist is represented.
keyPoints: Steven Soderbergh directed a new documentary about John Lennon using Meta AI tools
The film premieres at the Cannes Film Festival later in May 2026
It is one of the most high-profile AI reconstructions of a deceased music icon
The project raises big questions about using AI to represent artists who cannot consent
Meta gains significant cultural credibility through the collaboration with Soderbergh

articles.title: Lindsey Vonn Walks Met Gala 2026 Carpet Three Months After Nearly Losing Her Leg
articles.summary: Lindsey Vonn walked the 2026 Met Gala cream carpet in Thom Browne just three months after suffering a near-catastrophic leg injury at the Milano Cortina Olympics. The moment was her first major public appearance since the crash, and it hit differently knowing what she had just come through. For anyone who has followed Vonn's career, seeing her walk that carpet was genuinely emotional.
articles.content:
Lindsey Vonn has spent her entire career defying the odds, and Monday night at the 2026 Met Gala was another reminder of exactly why she is one of the most respected athletes in the world. Three months ago, at the Milano Cortina Winter Olympics, Vonn suffered an injury so severe that she nearly lost her leg. On Monday she walked the cream carpet in a Thom Browne look, on her own two feet, and the fashion world noticed.

For those who missed the news in February, Vonn was competing at the Milano Cortina Olympics, the Winter Games held in northern Italy, when she was involved in a serious crash. The details were alarming. The injury put her leg at real risk, and her recovery timeline was deeply uncertain. She is 41 years old and had already come back from multiple major injuries throughout her skiing career, including a brutal knee injury in 2013 that many thought would end her career entirely.

Choosing Thom Browne for this appearance was its own kind of statement. Thom Browne is an American designer known for sharp tailoring and theatrical runway presentations. His aesthetic is precise and structured, which made it a strong visual counterpoint to a moment that was really about raw human resilience. Vonn has always understood that how you show up matters.

The Met Gala is filled with celebrity appearances every year, but very few of them carry genuine emotional weight. Vonn's walk was one that did. This was not a comeback story with a neat bow on it. She is still recovering. But she showed up anyway, and walked the carpet, and that is the kind of moment that reminds you why sports and culture intersect in the way they do. It was one of the most talked-about appearances of the night, and rightly so.
keyPoints: Lindsey Vonn walked the 2026 Met Gala carpet in Thom Browne
This was her first major public appearance since nearly losing her leg at the Milano Cortina Olympics in February
Vonn is 41 and has a long history of recovering from serious injuries, including a major knee injury in 2013
The Milano Cortina Olympics were the 2026 Winter Games held in northern Italy
Her appearance was widely considered one of the most emotionally significant moments of the Met Gala night

articles.title: Google DeepMind UK Staff Vote to Unionize Over Military AI Deals
articles.summary: Workers at Google DeepMind's UK office have voted to unionize, with staff hoping to block the use of Google's AI models in military settings. The move reflects growing unease inside major AI labs about how their technology is being used. It is one of the most significant acts of worker organizing inside a leading AI research institution.
articles.content:
Staff at Google DeepMind's UK operation have voted to unionize, and the core issue driving it is military AI. Workers want a say in whether Google's artificial intelligence models are used in military settings, and they clearly feel that without formal organizing they have no real power to block that from happening. This is a big moment, not just for Google DeepMind but for the broader AI industry.

Google DeepMind is the AI research division that Google formed by merging its DeepMind lab (originally a London-based startup it acquired in 2014) with its internal Google Brain team. It is responsible for some of the most advanced AI research in the world, including work on models that underpin many of Google's products. The lab has long had a reputation for attracting idealistic researchers who care deeply about how AI develops and what it is used for.

The concern about military use is not abstract. Across the tech industry, there has been a running tension between AI companies pursuing lucrative government and defence contracts and the researchers who actually build these systems and worry about the consequences. Google itself faced a major internal revolt in 2018 over a Pentagon contract called Project Maven, which involved using AI to analyze drone footage. That protest led Google to pull out of Project Maven, showing that organized worker pressure can actually change corporate decisions.

For Google DeepMind staff, the decision to unionize is a way of formalizing that kind of pressure. If the union gains recognition and negotiating power, workers could potentially have a contractual say over which projects the lab takes on. That would be a genuinely unusual level of employee influence inside a company the size of Google.

This story matters well beyond Google DeepMind. AI is moving faster than regulations or public debate can keep up with, and many of the people who understand these systems best are the ones building them. When those people start organizing collectively to shape how the technology is deployed, it signals that the internal culture of AI labs is shifting. Whether Google responds by engaging seriously with the union or by pushing back hard will say a lot about how the wider industry handles this tension going forward.
keyPoints: Google DeepMind UK staff have voted to unionize
Workers want to prevent Google's AI models from being used in military settings
Google previously faced internal revolt over the Pentagon's Project Maven drone AI contract in 2018
Google DeepMind was formed by merging the London-based DeepMind lab with Google Brain
A recognized union could give workers a formal say over which projects the lab takes on

articles.title: Bad Bunny Shows Up to 2026 Met Gala as a Grandpa
articles.summary: Bad Bunny turned heads at the 2026 Met Gala by showing up transformed into an elderly version of himself, complete with wrinkles and grey hair. It was one of the most talked-about looks of the night, blending costume craft with a genuinely weird and funny concept. The Grammy winner basically gave everyone a preview of what he might look like fifty years from now.
articles.content:
Bad Bunny walked into the 2026 Met Gala and nobody was ready for it. The Puerto Rican superstar, who won Grammys and headlined the Super Bowl halftime show, showed up fully committed to a bit: he looked like an old man. We are talking wrinkles, grey hair, the whole thing. Not a subtle nod to ageing. A full transformation.

The Met Gala, held every year at the Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York, is basically the biggest fashion night of the year. Celebrities dress to a theme, and the looks get debated for days. Bad Bunny has never been someone who plays it safe on a red carpet, and 2026 was no exception.

What made this work is that Bad Bunny leaned all the way in. This was not a half-measure with a grey streak in his hair. The prosthetics and styling were detailed enough that the look genuinely read as a man in his seventies or eighties. The contrast between his actual age and the transformation was the whole joke, and it landed.

It is the kind of Met Gala moment that people screenshot and send to friends. Bad Bunny has spent years building a reputation as someone who refuses to be predictable, and showing up as his own future elderly self is exactly on brand for him.
keyPoints: Bad Bunny appeared at the 2026 Met Gala dressed as an elderly version of himself
The look included realistic wrinkles and grey hair
Bad Bunny is a Grammy winner and Super Bowl halftime performer
The Met Gala is held annually at the Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York
The transformation was detailed enough to genuinely read as decades of ageing

articles.title: China Deploys Humanoid Robot Police at Major Tourist Hotspot
articles.summary: China has rolled out a squad of humanoid police robots to manage crowds at one of its busiest tourist destinations. The robots represent a major step in blending AI-driven law enforcement with public crowd control. It is one of the most visible real-world deployments of humanoid robots in a policing role anywhere in the world.
articles.content:
China just put robot cops on the beat. A squad of humanoid police robots has been deployed at a major tourist hotspot in China to help manage the crowds that pour through the site every day. These are not little rolling kiosks or screens on wheels. These are full humanoid robots, built to look and move like people, now standing alongside human officers in a very public setting.

The decision makes sense when you think about the scale of crowd management China deals with at its top tourist sites. Millions of visitors pass through its busiest landmarks every year, and keeping order in those spaces is a massive logistical challenge. Humanoid robots can be stationed for hours without breaks, can be equipped with cameras and sensors, and can relay information to human officers in real time.

This is a big moment for robotics and for the conversation around AI in law enforcement. China has been investing heavily in both humanoid robot technology and AI-powered surveillance, and this deployment shows those two tracks are now merging in a very concrete way. Other countries are watching closely, because what gets tested in public spaces like this tends to spread.

The pushback question is already forming. Civil liberties groups and tech critics have long raised concerns about AI surveillance in public spaces, and putting that technology inside a humanoid robot that physically patrols crowds takes those concerns to a new level. For now, China is framing this as a crowd management tool, but the implications go well beyond tourist traffic.
keyPoints: China has deployed humanoid police robots at a major tourist hotspot to manage crowds
The robots are fully humanoid in design, not simple kiosks or wheeled units
This is one of the most prominent real-world uses of humanoid robots in a policing context globally
The deployment merges China's advances in humanoid robotics with its AI surveillance infrastructure
Critics are raising concerns about AI-powered robots being used in public law enforcement roles

articles.title: M.I.A. Kicked Off Kid Cudi Tour for Onstage Political Rants
articles.summary: M.I.A. has been removed from Kid Cudi's tour after repeatedly using her stage time to deliver right-wing political rants instead of performing. This is not a one-off incident — M.I.A. has been escalating her controversial statements for years, including comparing Alex Jones' Sandy Hook lies to celebrity culture back in 2022. Fans who bought tickets to see her open for Kid Cudi got something very different from what they paid for.
articles.content:
M.I.A. is off Kid Cudi's tour, and it is not because of a scheduling conflict or creative differences. She was removed because she kept using her opening set to go on political rants rather than play music. Audiences came to hear songs. They got lectures instead.

This is not a new pattern for M.I.A. Back in 2022, she drew widespread criticism when she compared Alex Jones spreading lies about the Sandy Hook school shooting to mainstream celebrity culture. Sandy Hook was one of the deadliest mass shootings in American history, and Alex Jones spent years falsely claiming it was staged, which led to him being sued and ordered to pay nearly $1.5 billion to victims' families. Framing his behaviour as comparable to normal celebrity PR moves was a significant moment in M.I.A.'s public shift toward far-right rhetoric.

Since then, M.I.A. has continued down that road, and the Kid Cudi tour removal seems to be the point where a fellow artist finally drew a hard line. Opening acts exist to warm up a crowd, not to alienate it. When your political tangents start overshadowing the headline act's show, the headline act gets to make a call.

It is a strange place for M.I.A. to land. She built her entire career on being a politically charged, anti-establishment voice. Songs like "Paper Planes" and her debut album "Arular" were rooted in commentary about war, displacement, and power. That she has migrated from those politics to echoing far-right talking points is a genuine cultural fall from grace, and this tour removal feels like the music world finally saying out loud what many have been thinking for a while.
keyPoints: M.I.A. was removed from Kid Cudi's tour for going on political rants during her opening sets
This follows years of M.I.A. embracing right-wing rhetoric, including a 2022 comment comparing Alex Jones' Sandy Hook lies to celebrity culture
Alex Jones was ordered to pay nearly $1.5 billion to Sandy Hook victims' families for spreading false claims about the shooting
M.I.A. built her career on left-leaning political music, making her current positions a sharp reversal
The removal signals the music community drawing a line on how far onstage political rants can go

articles.title: Samsung Family Pays Record $8B Tax, Calls It Their Duty
articles.summary: The founding family of Samsung has completed a record-breaking inheritance tax payment of 12 trillion South Korean won, roughly $8 billion, after the death of Samsung chairman Lee Kun-hee in 2020. The payment represents almost 50 percent of the total wealth passed on to them. The family publicly described paying the tax as their duty, setting a striking example at a time when billionaire tax avoidance dominates global headlines.
articles.content:
When Samsung chairman Lee Kun-hee died in October 2020, he left behind one of the largest private fortunes ever accumulated in South Korea. His family, including his wife Hong Ra-hee and children Lee Jae-yong, Lee Boo-jin, and Lee Seo-hyun, inherited that wealth along with a staggering tax bill. Now, five years later, they have finished paying it, and the final number is genuinely jaw-dropping.

The Samsung founding family paid 12 trillion South Korean won in inheritance tax, which works out to approximately $8 billion. That is not a typo. Eight billion dollars, paid to the South Korean government, making it the largest inheritance tax payment ever recorded in the country. To put it in perspective, that figure is almost 50 percent of the total value of what was passed down to them. They gave up nearly half their inheritance to the state, voluntarily and on time.

What makes this story cut through is not just the number. It is the attitude. The Samsung family publicly described the payment as their duty. In a world where the ultra-wealthy routinely employ armies of lawyers and accountants to minimise, defer, or avoid inheritance and estate taxes, the Samsung family did the opposite. They paid in full and said it was the right thing to do. South Korea has one of the highest inheritance tax rates in the world, with a top rate of 50 percent, and the Samsung family did not fight it.

Lee Jae-yong, who now leads Samsung Electronics as its executive chairman, has been a central figure in the process. Samsung Electronics is the world's largest maker of memory chips and one of the biggest consumer electronics companies on earth, so the eyes of the global business community were on how this succession would play out. The tax payment closes a major chapter in that transition and signals that Samsung intends to stay in South Korea's good graces as it navigates an increasingly competitive global tech landscape.

The broader context here is worth sitting with. Across Europe, the United States, and much of Asia, debates about wealth taxes and inheritance taxes are raging. Critics argue that dynastic wealth concentrates power and opportunity unfairly. Defenders of wealthy families argue that high taxes destroy businesses and push capital offshore. The Samsung family's decision to pay without drama does not settle that debate, but it does complicate the narrative that billionaires will always do whatever it takes to keep every dollar. Sometimes, apparently, they just pay the bill.
keyPoints: The Samsung founding family paid 12 trillion won, around $8 billion, in inheritance tax
The payment is the largest inheritance tax payment ever recorded in South Korea
The tax bill represented nearly 50 percent of the total wealth inherited after chairman Lee Kun-hee died in 2020
The family publicly called paying the tax their duty, standing out against global norms of billionaire tax avoidance
South Korea has a top inheritance tax rate of 50 percent, one of the highest in the world

articles.title: Electrical Fire Shuts Down Book of Mormon Indefinitely
articles.summary: An electrical fire at the Eugene O'Neill Theater in New York has forced Book of Mormon to close with no confirmed return date. The New York Fire Department confirmed that a room inside the theater sustained substantial damage. Producers have not announced when performances will resume.
articles.content:
Book of Mormon went dark on Broadway after an electrical fire broke out at the Eugene O'Neill Theater. The New York Fire Department confirmed the fire caused substantial damage to a room inside the venue. As of right now, producers have not given any timeline for when the show will be back.

Book of Mormon is one of Broadway's longest-running and most celebrated musicals. It was created by Trey Parker and Matt Stone, the same duo behind South Park, along with composer Robert Lopez. The show opened in 2011 and won nine Tony Awards, which are the top honours in American theater. It is known for its sharp and irreverent humour and has been a consistent box office hit for over a decade.

The Eugene O'Neill Theater is a historic Broadway venue that has hosted some of the biggest shows in New York. An electrical fire causing substantial damage is not a quick fix. Depending on what systems were affected, repairs could involve electrical rewiring, smoke damage restoration, and safety inspections before the city would allow audiences back inside.

For the cast, crew, and everyone who works behind the scenes, an indefinite closure is a serious disruption. Broadway productions run on tight schedules and the people who work them depend on consistent performances for their income. Fans who have tickets are now in a waiting game with no clear end date in sight.
keyPoints: An electrical fire at the Eugene O'Neill Theater has closed Book of Mormon indefinitely
The New York Fire Department confirmed substantial damage to a room inside the theater
Producers have not announced a return date for performances
Book of Mormon won nine Tony Awards after opening in 2011
The show was created by South Park's Trey Parker and Matt Stone alongside Robert Lopez

articles.title: Dolly Parton Cancels Las Vegas Residency Due to Health Issues
articles.summary: Dolly Parton has cancelled her Las Vegas residency, citing health problems that make performing unsafe for her right now. Parton explained it herself in characteristically honest and funny terms, saying she cannot be dizzy carrying banjos, guitars and such on five-inch heels. It is a rare step back for one of music's most enduring and hardworking performers.
articles.content:
Dolly Parton is stepping away from her Las Vegas residency, and she is being completely upfront about why. The country music legend said she is dealing with health issues that make the physical demands of a full residency show too risky to take on right now.

Parton put it perfectly in her own words: "I can't be dizzy carrying banjos, guitars, and such on five-inch heels." That single sentence tells you everything. Parton is 80 years old, she performs in towering heels, and she carries real instruments on stage. The concern is completely understandable, even if it stings to hear.

A Las Vegas residency is one of the most demanding formats in live music. Artists perform multiple nights a week for weeks or months at a time, often with elaborate staging and long set lists. For someone dealing with dizziness or balance issues, that kind of schedule is genuinely dangerous, not just uncomfortable. Parton made the right call.

Parton has been a constant in American music since the 1960s. She wrote Jolene and I Will Always Love You, the latter of which Whitney Houston turned into one of the best-selling singles of all time. Parton has also been a major philanthropist, donating over 200 million books to children through her Imagination Library program. She is not someone who cancels anything lightly.

Fans will obviously be disappointed, but the response online has been mostly warmth and support. People want Dolly Parton healthy and around for as long as possible. A cancelled residency is a small price to pay for that. There is no word yet on whether the residency will be rescheduled once Parton's health improves.
keyPoints: Dolly Parton has cancelled her Las Vegas residency due to health issues
Parton cited dizziness as the key concern, noting the danger of performing in five-inch heels while carrying instruments
Parton is 80 years old and has been one of music's most consistent live performers for decades
No rescheduled dates have been announced yet
The cancellation is rare for Parton, who is known for her tireless work ethic

articles.title: The UK Is Phasing Out Cigarettes. Will America Ever Follow?
articles.summary: The United Kingdom is actively phasing out smoking through a generational ban that will eventually make it illegal to sell cigarettes to anyone born after 2009, and The Atlantic is asking whether the United States will ever do the same. America still has around 28 million adult smokers, and tobacco's political and economic roots run far deeper in the US than in the UK. The gap between the two countries' approaches to smoking tells a much bigger story about how each nation thinks about personal freedom, public health, and corporate power.
articles.content:
The UK is not just discouraging smoking. It is legislating it out of existence. Under the Tobacco and Vapes Bill passed in the UK, anyone born on or after January 1, 2009 will never legally be able to buy cigarettes in England, no matter how old they get. The law creates a rolling smoke-free generation. It does not ban cigarettes outright today, but over time the legal buying age moves up every year until the market essentially disappears.

This is a genuinely bold policy move. Cigarettes kill around 80,000 people a year in the UK, and the government has decided that the long-term public health gain is worth the political fight. Former Prime Minister Rishi Sunak first pushed the bill, and the current Labour government under Keir Starmer carried it through. That kind of cross-party momentum is rare and says a lot about how seriously the UK now treats smoking as a public health crisis rather than a lifestyle choice.

In the United States the picture looks very different. About 28 million American adults still smoke, and cigarette companies have spent decades and billions of dollars making sure federal regulation stays limited. The FDA gained authority to regulate tobacco only in 2009, far later than health advocates had pushed for. Individual states have raised smoking ages and restricted where people can smoke, but a nationwide generational ban of the kind the UK just passed feels far off politically.

The deeper question The Atlantic is raising is about the American relationship with personal freedom. In the US, the argument that government should not tell people what they can put in their bodies carries enormous political weight, even when the thing in question kills roughly 480,000 Americans every year. That number makes tobacco the leading cause of preventable death in the country. The UK has essentially decided that statistic overrides the freedom argument. America has not gotten there yet.

What is also worth watching is how the UK's policy affects the vaping industry. The same Tobacco and Vapes Bill includes new restrictions on vaping products, particularly those marketed to young people. Vaping was originally promoted as a tool to help smokers quit, but it created a whole new generation of nicotine-dependent teenagers. The UK is trying to close that loophole at the same time it closes the cigarette one. Whether America addresses both issues together or keeps treating them separately will say a lot about where US health policy is actually headed.
keyPoints: The UK's Tobacco and Vapes Bill creates a generational ban, making it illegal to ever sell cigarettes to anyone born on or after January 1, 2009
Cigarettes kill around 80,000 people annually in the UK and roughly 480,000 in the US each year
The US has around 28 million adult smokers and federal tobacco regulation only began in 2009
The UK bill also restricts vaping products aimed at young people, addressing both issues simultaneously
America's strong cultural emphasis on personal freedom remains the biggest barrier to similar legislation

articles.title: Pussycat Dolls Cancel North American Reunion Tour Dates
articles.summary: The Pussycat Dolls have cancelled the entire North American leg of their reunion tour, calling it a difficult and heartbreaking decision. The group said they took an honest look at the run before pulling the plug. It is a major blow for fans who had been waiting years for the group to perform together again.
articles.content:
The Pussycat Dolls reunion is not happening in North America, at least not on this tour. The group announced they are cancelling the full North American leg of their reunion tour, describing it as a difficult and heartbreaking decision in a statement to fans. If you had tickets, this one stings.

The Pussycat Dolls originally broke up in 2010 after years of massive global success, with hits like Don't Cha and When I Grow Up making them one of the biggest pop acts of the mid-2000s. The reunion was first teased back in 2019 but got derailed by a combination of the pandemic, internal tensions involving lead singer Nicole Scherzinger, and legal disputes that dragged on for years. Getting the band back together has been a long and complicated road.

The group said they took an honest look at the North American run before making the call. That kind of language usually signals that ticket sales, logistics, or internal issues made the tour unworkable in its current form. They called it heartbreaking, which tells you this was not an easy exit. No specific rescheduled dates have been announced yet.

For fans, this is frustrating but sadly not shocking given how bumpy the Pussycat Dolls reunion journey has been. The question now is whether the North American dates get rescheduled or whether this is effectively the end of the road for this particular reunion attempt. The group has not ruled out future shows, but nothing is confirmed.
keyPoints: The Pussycat Dolls have cancelled the full North American leg of their reunion tour
The group called it a difficult and heartbreaking decision after taking an honest look at the run
The reunion has been in the works since 2019 but faced years of delays and legal disputes
No rescheduled North American dates have been announced
It is unclear whether the cancellation is permanent or a postponement

articles.title: Gap Co-Founder Doris Fisher Dies at 94
articles.summary: Doris Fisher, who co-founded Gap Inc. with her husband Don Fisher in 1969, died on Monday at the age of 94. She helped grow Gap from a single San Francisco storefront into a global retail empire worth $16 billion. Fisher was also a major art collector and philanthropist, and her influence on American fashion is hard to overstate.
articles.content:
Doris Fisher did not set out to build one of the most recognisable clothing brands on the planet. She and her husband Don just wanted a store that sold Levi's jeans in every size. That first Gap store opened in San Francisco in 1969, and it changed the way Americans thought about everyday dressing.

Under Doris Fisher's guidance, Gap grew from that single storefront into a global business generating $16 billion in revenue. Gap Inc. eventually expanded to include Banana Republic, Old Navy, and Athleta, reshaping the entire mid-range fashion market along the way. At its peak, Gap was the default uniform for a generation of Americans who wanted something clean, simple, and affordable.

Fisher was not just a retailer. She was a serious art collector, amassing one of the most significant private collections of contemporary art in the world. Works by Gerhard Richter, Ellsworth Kelly, and Richard Serra filled her San Francisco home. She later donated a major portion of the collection to the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art, cementing her legacy in the art world as much as in fashion.

Fisher is survived by her three sons, John, Robert, and William, who have all played roles in Gap Inc. at various points. She was 94. The fashion world has lost one of its true originals, someone who proved that a simple idea, done right, can clothe the world.
keyPoints: Doris Fisher co-founded Gap Inc. in San Francisco in 1969 with her husband Don Fisher
Gap grew under her leadership into a $16 billion global business
Gap Inc. also owns Banana Republic, Old Navy, and Athleta
Fisher was a major art collector who donated significant works to the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art
She died on Monday at the age of 94

articles.title: Employers Are Banning Smartphones at Work and Workers Are Pushing Back
articles.summary: A growing number of employers are confiscating personal smartphones during the workday, citing security risks and productivity concerns. Workers are not happy about it, and the policy is sparking real tension between employers and staff. The trend is being watched closely as companies try to reclaim focus in an always-on world.
articles.content:
Your boss might want your phone. Not to borrow it. To lock it away until you clock out. A growing wave of employers is banning personal smartphones during work hours, and the reasons they give are a mix of security fears and a genuine belief that phones are destroying productivity at work. It sounds extreme, but it is becoming more common across multiple industries.

The security argument is the one that is hardest to argue with. In environments where sensitive data, trade secrets, or client information are part of the job, a personal smartphone is essentially a tiny broadcast device sitting in your pocket. Employees can photograph documents, record conversations, or accidentally expose company systems to malware through personal apps. For industries like finance, defence contracting, manufacturing, and healthcare, those are not hypothetical risks.

The productivity case is where things get more contested. Employers who have implemented smartphone bans report that workers are more focused, meetings run faster, and the general noise level of distraction drops. That tracks with research showing the average person checks their phone dozens of times a day. But employees are pushing back hard, arguing that banning personal phones feels controlling, creates anxiety around family emergencies, and treats workers like children who cannot manage their own attention.

The pushback is significant enough that some companies are having to get creative. Some are offering dedicated break times for phone use, or setting up secure lockers where phones are stored but easily accessible in a genuine emergency. Others are drawing a line between banning phones on the production floor versus in common areas, trying to find a middle ground that does not feel punitive.

This is a trend worth watching because it sits at the intersection of workplace culture, personal autonomy, and a very real productivity crisis. Remote work blurred every line between personal and professional life, and now that many workers are back in offices, employers are trying to reassert focus. Smartphone bans are an aggressive move, but they signal just how serious the attention problem has become. Expect this conversation to get louder before it gets quieter.
keyPoints: More employers are banning personal smartphones during work hours over security and productivity concerns
Security risks like accidental data exposure and recording are a key driver in sensitive industries
Workers are pushing back, citing concerns about emergencies and feeling micromanaged
Some companies are using lockers and scheduled phone breaks as a compromise
The trend reflects a broader tension between personal autonomy and employer control over focus at work

articles.title: Mourinho's Benfica Go Unbeaten All Season but Still Lose the Title
articles.summary: Jose Mourinho's Benfica are on course to finish an entire Portuguese league season without losing a single game, yet they will still miss out on the Primeira Liga title. A rival club has simply accumulated more points, meaning Benfica's remarkable unbeaten run is not enough to take the trophy. It is one of the most unusual footnotes in modern European football.
articles.content:
Jose Mourinho has done a lot of things in football, but he has never had a season quite like this one. His Benfica side have gone the entire 2025/26 Primeira Liga season without losing, and they are still not going to win the league. That is a genuinely rare thing to be able to say.

The reason is simple. Going unbeaten does not automatically mean you win the most games. Drawing matches instead of winning them costs points, and it appears Benfica drew enough times that a rival club pulled ahead on the table. In European football, three points go to the winner of a match and one point to each side in a draw. Run up enough draws and an opponent who wins more games will finish above you even if they lose a few.

For Mourinho, this is a complicated story. He is one of the most decorated managers in football history, having won league titles in Portugal, England, Italy, and Spain. He returned to Benfica, the Lisbon club where he had an early stint as a coach, with a lot of expectation. Going unbeaten is a genuine achievement. Finishing second despite that unbeaten run is going to sting.

It also adds a strange chapter to Mourinho's later career narrative. After high-profile exits from Tottenham Hotspur, AS Roma, and other clubs, his reputation as a manager who delivers trophies had taken some hits. This Benfica season is hard to categorise. Impressive on one level. Frustrating on another. Football has a way of being both at the same time.
keyPoints: Jose Mourinho's Benfica will finish the 2025/26 Primeira Liga season unbeaten but without the title
A rival club accumulated more points, likely by winning more matches while Benfica drew too many
Benfica are chasing their 39th Portuguese league title
Mourinho has previously won league titles in Portugal, England, Italy, and Spain
The result adds another complicated chapter to Mourinho's recent managerial career

articles.title: Phone Bans in Schools Boosted Wellbeing But Not Test Scores
articles.summary: A new study has found that cellphone bans in US schools did not lead to any improvement in students' academic test scores. However, students themselves reported feeling better overall in the years after bans were introduced. The findings complicate the popular argument that banning phones in schools will automatically make kids smarter.
articles.content:
Phones in schools have been one of the biggest debates in education for years. Dozens of US states and school districts have moved to ban or restrict cellphones in classrooms, with the main promise being that without the distraction, kids will learn more and score better on tests. A new study suggests that promise has not been delivered.

The research found no meaningful improvement in student test scores following cellphone bans across US schools. This is a pretty significant finding because academic performance is usually the headline argument politicians and school boards lean on when pushing these bans. If the grades are not going up, the core justification takes a serious hit.

But here is where it gets more complicated. Students themselves reported improvements in their overall wellbeing in the years after bans were put in place. That means something real is happening on the ground, just not the thing everyone was measuring. Kids feeling better at school matters enormously even if it does not show up immediately in test results. Mental health, sense of belonging, and day-to-day happiness are not small things.

This puts the phone ban debate in a genuinely interesting spot. If the goal is better grades, the evidence so far says bans are not the answer. But if the goal is happier, less anxious students, there may actually be something to them. The tricky part is that policymakers tend to sell these bans on academic grounds, which is exactly where the evidence is weakest.

What this study really calls for is a more honest conversation about what we actually want from phone bans and how we measure success. Better test scores and better wellbeing are both worthwhile goals, but they are not the same thing, and conflating them has muddied the whole debate. More research tracking long-term outcomes will be needed before anyone can say definitively whether these bans are worth it.
keyPoints: A new study found cellphone bans in US schools did not improve student test scores
Students did report improvements in overall wellbeing after bans were introduced
The findings challenge the most common argument used to justify phone bans in schools
The gap between academic results and student wellbeing complicates policymaking
Researchers say more long-term data is needed to reach firmer conclusions
articles.wikiSearchQuery: cellphone ban schools United States education policy

articles.title: Santana Singer Alex Ligertwood Dead At 79
articles.summary: Alex Ligertwood, the Scottish vocalist best known as one of Santana's lead singers, passed away in his sleep at home. His daughter announced his death on social media. Ligertwood had multiple stints fronting Santana and left a lasting mark on the band's classic era.
articles.content:
Alex Ligertwood, the Scottish singer who became one of the most recognisable voices in Santana's long history, has died at the age of 79. His daughter shared the news on social media, confirming that Ligertwood died peacefully in his sleep at home. The music world is pausing to remember a man who brought real soul to one of rock's most beloved bands.

Ligertwood was born in Scotland but built his career across multiple continents, eventually landing one of the most coveted roles in rock, fronting Santana. He had several stints as the band's lead singer, meaning he was not just a one-album presence but a genuine long-term voice for the group. Santana, the band formed by guitarist Carlos Santana in San Francisco in the late 1960s, is famous for blending rock, Latin rhythms, and blues into a sound that still fills stadiums today.

For fans who grew up with Santana's output through the 1980s and beyond, Ligertwood's voice is inseparable from some of their most cherished memories. He had a range and warmth that suited the band's expansive sound perfectly. His contributions helped Santana stay vital across decades when many of their contemporaries had faded.

Ligertwood was 79 years old. No further details about the exact date or location of his passing have been shared by his family beyond the social media announcement from his daughter. Tributes from the music community are already coming in for a singer who gave a lot of himself to the music and asked for very little credit in return.
keyPoints: Alex Ligertwood died in his sleep at home at age 79
His daughter confirmed the news on social media
Ligertwood served multiple stints as lead singer of Santana
He was a Scottish musician who became central to Santana's sound across decades
